Example 1: If a creature had both ADV and DisADV then that would become a standard roll of a single d20. If you had DisADV 3 and also had ADV, then one of the ADV would cancel out one of the DisADV and you would be left with DisADV 2.

Example 2: If a creature has both DisADV 3 and and ADV 2 on a Check, two of the ADV cancel out two of the DisADV, leaving only one DisADV remaining. The Check is then made with DisADV 1, which is just regular DisADV (rolling 2 dice and taking the lower number).

DC Tip: Basically just subtract any ADV or DisADV from each other and see what you are left with, and if the answer is ever 0, then you just make a standard d20 roll.

heading: Variable ADV & DisADV
text:
## Variable ADV & DisADV

Gaining ADV and DisADV on Checks made against other creatures is very common. Sometimes you might have to make a Check against a group of creatures where you have ADV or DisADV on the Check against some of the targets but not all of them. When that happens, follow these steps below:

1. Make a Check against all targets you don't have ADV or DisADV against by rolling a single d20 and adding any relevant bonuses you have on the Check against the targets.
2. For any additional targets you have ADV or DisADV against, roll a second d20 and compare it to the first d20 you already rolled. For all targets you have ADV against, choose the higher number rolled, and for DisADV choose the lower number rolled.
3. If you have ADV/DisADV 2 or higher against any additional targets, repeat Step 2 for each additional dice you have to roll until you resolve the outcome of your Check against all targets.

Example: A Wizard targets 3 creatures with a Fireball Spell. It doesn't have ADV against the first target (rolls 1d20), has ADV against the second target (rolls 2d20), and ADV 2 against the third target (rolls 3d20). The Wizard rolls a d20 and uses it to determine the outcome of its Spell Check against the first target. It then rolls another d20 and compares it to the first d20 (total of 2d20s) to determine the outcome of its Spell Check against the second target. It then rolls another d20 and compares it to the first 2 d20s (total of 3d20s) to determine the outcome of its Spell Check against the third target.

<!-- page: 31 -->

heading: Multiple Check Penalty
text:
## Multiple Check Penalty

During Combat, each time a creature makes the same Check before the end of its turn, they suffer stacking DisADV on that type of Check, such as specific Flat Attribute Checks (Flat Might, Flat Agility, Flat Charisma, Flat Intelligence), Attack Checks, Spell Checks, and specific Skill Checks (Stealth, Athletics, Influence, etc.), and specific Trade Checks (Theatre, Blacksmithing, Leatherworking, etc.). This penalty only applies to Checks made during the creature's turn, and doesn't apply to Reactions taken on another creature's turn (unless otherwise stated).

- 1st Attempt: Normal roll.
- 2nd Attempt: DisADV 1 (roll 2 dice and take the lowest result).
- 3rd Attempt: DisADV 2 (roll 3 dice and take the lowest result).
- 4th Attempt: DisADV 3 (roll 4 dice and take the lowest result).

Example: If a creature casts a Spell by making a Spell Check, its first attempt would be a normal roll, but subsequent Spell Checks would have stacking DisADV (as shown above).

DC Tip: The Multiple Check Penalty does not apply outside of Combat. See Combat Resources for more information on how Actions are taken outside of Combat.

Multiple Action Penalties: Some Actions have penalties when you take it multiple times, such as the Help Action. These penalties count as the Multiple Check Penalty for the purposes of game mechanics that apply to Multiple Check Penalties.

heading: Check & Save Outcomes
text: ## Check & Save Outcomes

heading: Critical Success & Failure
text:
## Critical Success & Failure

When a creature rolls a 20 on a d20, the Check or Save gains a Critical Success. When a creature rolls a 1 on a d20, the Check or Save suffers a Critical Failure. A Critical Success or Critical Failure can affect the outcome in different ways, as determined by the type of Check or Save being made.

heading: Attack, Martial, and Spell Checks
text: ## Attack, Martial, and Spell Checks

heading: Critical Success
text:
## Critical Success

Damage: When a creature gains a Critical Success on an Attack, Martial, or Spell Check that deals damage, the Attack is considered a Critical Hit (automatically hits and deals +2 damage).

Effect: When a creature gains a Critical Success on an Attack, Martial, or Spell Check that doesn't deal damage, the Check automatically succeeds even if the result would normally fail.

heading: Critical Failure
text:
## Critical Failure

When a creature gains a Critical Failure on an Attack, Martial, or Spell Check, it becomes Exposed against the next Attack made against it before the start of its next turn. They also suffer additional penalties depending on if the Check deals damage or not.

DC Tip: For Critical Failures, the GM could change the Condition to Hindered (your next Attack Check has DisADV) instead of Exposed at their discretion.

Damage: When a creature suffers a Critical Failure on an Attack, Martial, or Spell Check that deals damage, the Attack automatically misses even if the result would normally hit.

Effect: When a creature gains a Critical Failure on an Attack, Martial, or Spell Check that doesn't deal damage, the Check automatically fails even if the result would normally succeed.

<!-- page: 32 -->

heading: Saves, Skill Checks, & Trade Checks
text: ## Saves, Skill Checks, & Trade Checks

heading: Critical Success
text:
## Critical Success

When a creature gains a Critical Success on a Save, Skill Check, or Trade Check, the Check or Save automatically succeeds even if the result would normally fail.

heading: Critical Failure
text:
## Critical Failure

When a creature suffers a Critical Failure on a Save, Skill Check, or Trade Check, the Check or Save automatically fails even if the result would normally succeed.

DC Tip: The GM should avoid asking PCs to make Checks where success shouldn't be possible. If a PC ties a heavy chain around a building, it shouldn't be possible for them to succeed on a Might Check to drag the building. Conversely, the GM should avoid asking PCs to make Checks where failure shouldn't be possible, like asking a PC to make a Might Check pick up a pebble. Unless the circumstances introduce the possibility of success or failure, try to refrain from asking for Checks for activities that are impossible to succeed or fail. Mistakes are going to happen and GMs might ask for a roll when they shouldn't have. The final outcome of a Critical Success or Critical Failure are up to the GM, and PCs should respect that.

heading: Contested Critical Outcomes
text:
## Contested Critical Outcomes

When multiple creatures gain a Critical Success in a contest (or all creatures suffer a Critical Failure), the creature with the highest total result wins the contest.

heading: Degrees of Success & Failure
text: ## Degrees of Success & Failure

heading: Degrees of Success
text:
## Degrees of Success

If a creature succeeds on a Check or Save by 5 or more, they may gain additional benefits beyond those of a normal success. The Check or Save will include a success statement, such as Success, Success (5), Success (10), or Success (each 5).

heading: Success
text:
## Success

If the success statement says Success then the creature gains the listed benefits if the result of its Check or Save is equal to or higher than the opposing DC, Check, or Save.

heading: Success (each 5)
text:
## Success (each 5)

If a success statement says Success (each 5) then the creature gains the listed benefits for every 5 the result of its Check or Save is above the opposing DC, Check, or Save. Unless stated otherwise, the creature can gain the additional benefits multiple times.

Example: A Spell pushes a target back 3 Spaces on a successful Spell Check, but also includes the following success statement: "Success (each 5): +1 Space." That means that each 5 points the Spell Check is above the opposing DC, Check, or Save, the target is pushed 1 additional Space.

heading: Success (5) & Success (10)
text:
## Success (5) & Success (10)

If a success statement says Success (5) then the creature gains the listed benefits when they succeed by 5 or more above the opposing DC, Check, or Save. Moreover, if the success statement says Success (10) then they also gain the listed benefits when they succeed by 10 or more above the opposing DC, Check, or Save. This can continue on to Success (15) and further.

Example: A Spell increases a creature's Speed by 1 on a successful Spell Check, but has the following success statements: "Success (5): Ignore Difficult Terrain," and "S "Success (10): Immune to Slowed." That means that the creature's Speed is increased by 1 and ignores Difficult Terrain if its Spell Check is 5 above the Spell's DC, and the creature is also immune to the Slowed Condition if its Spell Check is 10 above the Spell's DC.

heading: Degrees of Failure
text:
## Degrees of Failure

If a creature fails on a Check or Save by 5 or more, they may suffer additional penalties beyond those of a normal failure. The Check or Save will include a failure statement, such as Failure, Failure (5), Failure (10), or Failure (each 5).

heading: Failure
text:
## Failure

If a failure statement says Failure then the creature suffers the listed penalties if the result of its Check or Save is below than the opposing DC, Check, or Save.

heading: Failure (each 5)
text:
## Failure (each 5)

If a failure statement says Failure (each 5) then the creature suffers the listed penalties for every 5 the result of its Check or Save is below the opposing DC, Check, or Save. Unless stated otherwise, the creature can suffer the additional penalties multiple times.

Example: A trap deals 4 Fire damage to a target on a failed Save, but also includes the following failure statement: " Failure (each 5): +1 damage." That means that each 5 points the Save is below the Trap's Save DC, the target takes an additional +1 Fire damage.

heading: Failure (5) & Failure (10)
text:
## Failure (5) & Failure (10)

If a failure statement says Failure (5) then the creature suffers the listed penalties when they fail by 5 or more below the opposing DC, Check, or Save. Moreover, if the failure statement says Failure (10) then they also suffer the listed penalties when they fail by 10 or more below the opposing DC, Check, or Save. This can continue on to Failure (15) and further.

Example: A Curse imposes the Blinded Condition on a failed Save, but has the following failure statements: "Failure (5): Deafened," and "Failure (10): Doomed." That means that the creature is both Blinded and Deafened if its Save is 5 below the Save DC, and the creature is also Doomed 1 if its Save is 10 below the Save DC.

<!-- page: 34 -->

heading: Attacks & Defenses
text: ## Attacks & Defenses

heading: Attacks
text:
## Attacks

When a creature makes an Attack, it can do so by choosing a target within the Attack's Range (Melee, Ranged, or Area) and by making an Attack Check. How the Attack is resolved is determined by its Type and its Range.

heading: Attack Types
text:
## Attack Types

There are 2 types of Attacks: Martial and Spell.

heading: Martial Attacks
text:
## Martial Attacks

A creature makes a Martial Attack when they make an Attack Check using a Weapon or Unarmed Strike against a target's Defense.

Unarmed Strike: A Martial Attack made using any part of a creature's own body, such as a Punch, Elbow, Kick, Knee, or Headbutt.

Weapon Attack: A Martial Attack made using a Weapon, such as an Axe, Sword, or Bow. You can draw a Weapon as part of an Attack made using it.

heading: Spell Attacks
text:
## Spell Attacks

A creature makes a Spell Attack when they make an Attack Check using a Spell or similar ability (such as the Breath Weapon Trait from the Dragonborn Ancestry) against a target's Defense (see the "Spellcaster Chapter" on page 59 for more information).

heading: Attack Ranges
text:
## Attack Ranges

Attacks have 3 types of ranges: Melee, Ranged, or Area.

heading: Melee Attack
text:
## Melee Attack

A Melee Attack is an Attack with a range of Melee. A creature's Melee Range is 1 Space, unless otherwise stated.

heading: Ranged Attack
text:
## Ranged Attack

A Ranged Attack is an Attack with a range specified in a number Spaces (such as 10 Spaces). The Range is determined by the type of Weapon it's Attacking with or by consulting the ability used to make the Ranged Attack.

Close Quarters: You have DisADV on Ranged Attacks if you're within the Melee Range of at least 1 enemy, unless that enemy is Incapacitated (see the Disadvantage and Conditions sections for more information).

heading: Weapon Ranges
text:
## Weapon Ranges

A Weapon that can be used to make a Ranged Attack has a Range shown in parentheses after its Ammo, Toss, or Thrown property (see "Equipment" on page 164) for more information).

Normal Range: The first number in the parentheses is a Weapon's normal range. You can make an attack against any target within this range.

Long Range: The second number in the parentheses is a Weapon's long range. You can make an attack with DisADV against targets within this range. You can't make attacks against targets beyond your Weapon's long range.

Example: A Short Bow with a range of (15/45) means that it can be used to make attacks against targets between 1 and 15 Spaces, attacks with DisADV against targets between 16 and 45 Spaces, and can't attack targets that are 46 Spaces or farther away.

heading: Area Attack
text:
## Area Attack

A creature makes an Area Attack when they make an Attack against targets that are within a specified area (such as a Cone, Line, or Sphere) covered by the Attack. The type and size of the area is determined by the Attack.

DC Tip: The Close Quarters penalty doesn't apply to Area Attacks.

heading: Determining if an Attack Hits
text:
## Determining if an Attack Hits

When a creature makes an Attack, the result of the Attack Check is compared to the target's Defense to determine whether or not the Attack Hits the target. The Attack Hits if the result of the Attack Check is equal to or greater than the target's Defense.

Example: A Level 4 Champion's highest Attribute is a 3 (Prime Modifier) and their Combat Mastery is 2 (1/2 its level). When the Champion makes an Attack Check using a Longsword, they roll a d20 and add 5 (3 + 2). If they roll a 10 on the d20, the result is 15 (10 + 5). If the target's PD is 15 or lower, the Attack Hits, otherwise it Misses.

heading: Defenses
text:
## Defenses

When a creature makes an Attack to attempt to deal damage to a target, the creature's Check is compared against the value of one of the target's Defenses (Precision Defense or Area Defense) to determine if the Attack hits.

heading: Precision Defense
text:
## Precision Defense

Precision Defense (PD) measures a creature's ability to avoid attacks that can be dodged, such as a precise strike or a ranged projectile. It reflects a combination of reflexes and tactical foresight, making it a crucial defense against single-target assaults.

heading: PD Calculation
text:
## PD Calculation

Your PD is equal to 8 plus your Combat Mastery, Agility, and Intelligence. If you are wearing Armor or wielding a Shield (see "Equipment" on page 164), you also add their respective bonuses to your PD.

- Agility: Represents your reaction speed and physical swiftness, allowing you to evade incoming attacks.
- Intelligence: Reflects your ability to anticipate danger and position yourself wisely, giving you the time needed to react.

heading: PD Formula
text:
## PD Formula

8 + Combat Mastery + Agility

+ Intelligence + Bonuses

heading: Area Defense
text:
## Area Defense

Area Defense (AD) measures a creature's ability to withstand attacks that can't be avoided, such as explosions, shockwaves, or other widespread effects. Instead of relying on dodging, Area Defense reflects raw endurance, resilience, and sheer force of will to endure the assault.

heading: AD Calculation
text:
## AD Calculation

Your AD is equal to 8 plus your Combat Mastery, Might, and Charisma. If you are wearing Armor or wielding a Shield (see "Equipment" on page 164), you also add their respective bonuses to your AD.

- Might: Represents your physical toughness and endurance, allowing you to absorb and resist bodily harm.
- Charisma: Reflects your inner resolve and commanding presence, reinforcing your ability to withstand mental and supernatural assaults.

heading: AD Formula
text:
## AD Formula

8 + Combat Mastery + Might

+ Charisma + Bonuses

heading: Health Points & Death ' s Door
text: ## Health Points & Death ' s Door

heading: Health Points
text:
## Health Points

Health Points (HP) are a game statistic representing a creature's ability to sustain damage. The stronger the creature the more HP it generally has, signifying the greater difficulty in defeating it.

Example: A Commoner has 4 HP, a Mastiff has 3 HP, a Cat has 2 HP and a Rat has 1 HP.

DC Tip: Most damage a creature takes is nonlethal–it just "wears them down" until they get closer and closer to Death's Door. Lighter hits might graze you, while bigger hits knock you off your feet. The description and roleplay of how damage is taken can change depending on its source and the situation, but Health Points represent how much of a beating you can take before you die.

heading: Health Points at 1st Level
text:
## Health Points at 1st Level

A PC has a number of Health Points equal to Class HP + Might. A PC might also gain additional HP from its Ancestry, such the Dwarf Trait Tough (+1 HP) or the Elf Trait Frail (-2 HP).

heading: Health Point Formula
text:
## Health Point Formula

Class HP + Might + Ancestry HP (if any)

Example: A PC picks the Champion Class at Level 1 and starts with a Might of 3 (the highest possible at Level 1). The Champion Class grants a base of 8 HP. The PC then adds +3 HP from their Might to get a total of 11 HP (8 + 3). If any of their Ancestry Traits increase or decrease their HP, they would add those to the total to determine their starting HP.

heading: Temporary Health Points
text:
## Temporary Health Points

Temporary Health Points (Temp HP) are additional Health Points that persist for a limited duration. When you take damage, your Temp HP is reduced first, and then any remaining damage carries over to reduce your HP.

Stacking: Temp HP doesn't stack. If you gain Temp HP from different effects, you choose which effect to gain Temp HP from, and ignore the Temp HP granted by the other effects.

Duration: Temp HP lasts until you complete a Long Rest or its reduced to 0 by taking damage. An effect that grants Temp HP may state a shorter duration, such as 1 minute, until you complete a Quick Rest or Short Rest, or some other duration. When the duration expires, all Temp HP gained immediately vanish.

<!-- page: 35 -->

heading: Health Thresholds
text:
## Health Thresholds

Certain game mechanics affect creatures while their current HP is within certain thresholds. These HP thresholds are determined before any temporary changes to your HP maximum, such as from the Doomed Condition.

- Bloodied: HP is equal to or lower than 1/2 its maximum HP.
- Well-Bloodied: HP is equal to or lower than 1/4 its maximum HP.
- Death's Door: HP is equal to or lower than 0 HP, to a minimum of its Death Threshold.

A creature's Death Threshold is equal to 0 HP minus its Prime Modifier and its Combat Mastery.

DC Tip: If a creature is on Death's Door, they are still considered to be Bloodied and Well-Bloodied, because their HP is still below 1/2 their maximum (Bloodied) and 1/4 their maximum (Well-Bloodied).

Example: A creature with a maximum HP of 8 is considered Bloodied when its HP is 4 or lower, Well-Bloodied when its HP is 2 or lower, and on Death's Door when its HP is 0 or lower. If its Prime Modifier is 3 and its Combat Mastery is 1, the creature's Death Threshold is -4 HP. That creature dies if its HP is ever reduced to -4 HP or lower.

heading: Death ' s Door
text:
## Death ' s Door

When a PC is reduced to 0 HP they're on "Death's Door" and fighting for their life. This is represented in whatever way the player wants to describe this state looks like for their character. They are NOT unconscious, they are in a heavily weakened state about to die if something is not done SOON! They could be prone crawling on the ground, holding themselves up with their sword, or spitting out blood as they hold on for dear life.

heading: Entering Death ' s Door
text:
## Entering Death ' s Door

When you're reduced to 0 HP or lower, you gain Exhaustion 1. Until you are restored to 1 HP or higher, you suffer the following effects:

- Action Point Limit: Your current and maximum AP are reduced by 3.
- Death Save: You must make a DC 10 Death Save at the start of each of your turns.

DC Tip: You basically can only spend 1 AP per round of Combat, either on your turn or as a Reaction. This usually happens while on Death's Door.

heading: Death Saves
text:
## Death Saves

When a creature makes a Death Save, it rolls a d20 to determine if it Succeeds or Fails the Save.

heading: Success
text:
## Success

- Success: No change.
- Success (5): You regain 1 HP.
- Critical Success: You are restored to 1 HP.

heading: Failure
text:
## Failure

- Failure: You gain Bleeding 1.
- Failure (5): You gain Bleeding 2 instead.
- Critical Failure: You also fall Unconscious until you're restored to 1 HP or higher.

DC Tip: If you are on Death's Door outside of Combat, you repeat the Death Save every 12 seconds instead (5 times per minute).

heading: Death’s Door Sidebar
text: ## Death’s Door Sidebar

heading: Bleeding X (Condition)
text:
## Bleeding X (Condition)

<!-- page: 36 -->
You take X True damage at the start of each of your turns.

Ending Bleeding: All stacks of the Condition end when you're subjected to an effect that restores your HP. Alternatively, a creature can attempt to remove 1 or more stacks of the Condition by taking the Medicine Action.

heading: Medicine (Action)
text:
## Medicine (Action)

You can spend 1 AP to touch a creature and tend to its wounds. Make a DC 10 Medicine Check. Success: You end 1 stack of Bleeding on the target. Success (each 5): You end an additional stack of Bleeding.

heading: Medicine Kit (Equipment)
text:
## Medicine Kit (Equipment)

You can spend 1 Charge and take the Object Action to treat a Bloodied creature's wounds (see Medicine Kit for more information). Make a DC 10 Medicine Check. Success: Target creature regains 1 HP. Success (each 5): The creature regains +1 HP.

heading: NPC's on Death ' s Door
text:
## NPC's on Death ' s Door

Monsters and NPCs normally die when they're reduced to 0 HP, however the Game Master can choose to implement Death's Door on special enemies. They would drop down to 1 AP and be on Death's Door in that same weakened state, leaving a window for them to still talk to the party and get out any last words before someone puts them out of their misery.

<!-- page: 39 -->

heading: Damage
text: ## Damage

heading: Degrees of Damage
text:
## Degrees of Damage

Each Attack has a base amount of damage that's dealt on a Hit. This value is listed alongside the Weapon (see "Equipment" on page 133) or in the Spell's description (see "Spellcaster Chapter" on page 59). If the Check beats the targets Defense by 5 or more, the target takes extra damage and may suffer additional effects (see "Check & Save Outcomes" on page 31 for more information).

heading: Hit & Miss
text:
## Hit & Miss

When you make a Check against a target's Defense, the Check is considered a Miss if the result is below the target's Defense and it's considered a Hit if the result is equal to or above the target's Defense.

heading: Hit Success (each 5)
text:
## Hit Success (each 5)

An Attack deals +1 additional damage for every 5 the result of the Check is above the target's Defense. If the result of the Check is 5 or more above the target's Defense, it is considered a Heavy Hit. If the result of the Check is 10 or more above the target's Defense, it is considered a Brutal Hit. Brutal Hits are considered Heavy Hits for purposes of effects that trigger on a Heavy Hit.

DC Tip: Heavy Hits are 5 over, Brutal Hits are 10 over, but if a Character ever manages to get 15 over a target's Defense I call this Beyond Brutal. Then I count by 5s to see how many instances of +1 bonus damage they get.

heading: Degrees of Damage Table
text:
## Degrees of Damage Table

The table below shows how the damage of an Attack can change as determined by the result of the Check.

| Check vs Defense | Result | Damage |
|--------------------|------------|-----------------|
| Lower | Miss | No Damage |
| Equal or greater | Hit | Base Damage |
| +5 or greater | Heavy Hit | Base Damage + 1 |
| +10 or greater | Brutal Hit | Base Damage + 2 |

heading: Critical Hits
text:
## Critical Hits

When you roll a 20 on a d20 for an Attack, it's considered a Critical Hit. A Critical Hit automatically Hits the target, bypasses Damage Reduction, and deals an additional +2 damage.

DC Tip: An Attack can score a Critical Hit without the result of the Check being 5 or more above the target's Defense. When that happens, the Check is considered a Critical Hit and the target simply takes the base damage +2, however, this scenario is rare. A Critical Hit will normally result in a Check that's 5 or even 10 above the target's Defense, causing the target to take +2 damage from the Critical Hit plus +1 additional damage for every 5 the result is higher than its Defense.

heading: Damage Calculation Sidebar
text:
## Damage Calculation Sidebar

This is a way for both Players and Game Masters to add up their damage in a way that's simple and quick. Once you Hit with an Attack, the GM will tell you if the Attack was a Heavy Hit or higher. This helps you determine how much additional damage your Attack deals (see the process shown below). The GM will apply any applicable Resistances or Vulnerabilities to determine the total amount of damage the target takes (see "Bonus Damage" on page 38 for more on the target reducing damage).

heading: Adding Damage Order
text:
## Adding Damage Order

Add up the damage totals in the following order:

4. Base Damage: Start with the Base Damage of the Attack.
5. Heavy & Critical Hits: Add any bonus damage from Critical Hits or Heavy Hits and higher.
6. Action Points: Add any bonus damage from Action Points spent (Maneuvers, Class Features, AP Enhancements, etc.).
7. Situational Bonuses: Add any bonus damage from situational bonuses (Class Features, Magic Items, etc.).

DC Tip: Starting with the Base Damage is a great foundation, and getting the Heavy Hit + out of the way first is nice. You'll get better at all the "by 5's" math over time! Then you add any damage gained by spending AP, then any passives. This creates a great "checkpoin" system for you to mentally navigate and keep things simple and quick.

Example: A Rogue attacks a beast with his Shortsword (we start off with a Base Damage of 1). They get a Brutal Hit and gain +2 bonus damage on their hit (1 + 2 = 3). They target has a condition on it which increases their damage by +1 (1 + 2 + 1 = 4). So now their total damage is 5 (1 Base + 2 Brutal + 1 Cheap Shot = 4 Total).

heading: Damage Types
text:
## Damage Types

There are 12 different damage types in the game, as seen in the list below. Each damage type represents a different method for harming a creature.

Bludgeoning Damage: Things that bludgeon you, like a hammer or fist.

Piercing Damage: Things that pierce you, like an arrow or fangs.

Slashing Damage: Things that slash you, like a blade or claw.

Fire Damage: Things that burn you, like fire or other forms of intense heat.

Cold Damage: Things that freeze you. like ice or intense cold.

Lightning Damage: Things that shock you, like lightning or other forms of electricity.

Poison Damage: Things that poison you, like toxins.

Corrosion Damage: Things that decay and wither you, like acids and rust effects.

Psychic Damage: Things that assault your mind, like psychic attacks.

Radiant Damage: Things that singe you, like holy light, lasers, and radiation.

Umbral Damage: Things that harm your soul, like dark or eldritch power.

True Damage: A special type of damage that can't be reduced by any means.

heading: Damage Type Categories
text:
## Damage Type Categories

Damage types (except True damage) are organized into the following categories: Physical, Elemental, and Mystical.

Physical Damage: Includes Bludgeoning, Piercing, and Slashing damage.

Elemental Damage: Includes Cold, Corrosion, Fire, Lightning, and Poison.

Mystical Damage: Includes Psychic, Radiant, and Umbral.

heading: Damage Vulnerability, Resistance, & Immunity
text:
## Damage Vulnerability, Resistance, & Immunity

Resistance is a way for creatures to reduce the damage they take, while Vulnerability increases damage taken. The source of what gives them this ability could come from a natural ability they have, a Class Feature, Magic Items, etc. Here are the different ways that these different effects work:

heading: Damage Resistance
text:
## Damage Resistance

Resistance (X): Damage taken of the specified type is reduced by X each time the creature takes damage of that type.

Example: If you had Fire Resistance (2) and took 7 Fire damage, you would instead take 5 Fire damage (you subtract 2 from the Fire damage taken, so 7–2 = 5).

Resistance (Half): Damage taken of the specified type is halved (minimum of 1 damage reduced).

Example 1: If you had Fire Resistance (Half) and took 7 Fire damage, you would instead take 4 Fire damage (half of 7 is 3.5, rounded up is 4).

Example 2: If you had Radiant Resistance and took 1 Radiant damage, the damage would be halved to 0.5 and rounded back up to 1 damage taken. However, since Resistance reduces damage by a minimum of 1, the damage would be reduced to 0.

heading: Damage Immunity
text:
## Damage Immunity

Damage taken of the specified type is reduced to 0.

Example: If you had Cold Immunity and were dealt 7 Cold damage, you would take 0 damage instead.

heading: Damage Vulnerability
text:
## Damage Vulnerability

Vulnerability (X): Increase damage taken by X each time the creature takes damage of that type.

Example: If you had Poison Vulnerability (2) and took 3 Poison damage, you would instead take 5 Poison damage (you add 2 to the Poison damage taken, so 3 + 2 = 5).

Vulnerability (Double): Increases the Damage taken by 2 times of the specified type.

Example: If a Werewolf has Vulnerability (Double) to Silvered Weapons and takes 5 damage from an Attack, they would instead take 10 damage (5 x 2 = 10).

heading: Damage Reduction
text:
## Damage Reduction

Damage Reduction (DR) grants Damage Resistance (Half) against Attacks, except against Critical Hits and Heavy Hits (or higher). There are 3 types of Damage Reduction:

Physical Damage Reduction: PDR protects you from the following damage types: Bludgeoning, Piercing, and Slashing.

Elemental Damage Reduction: EDR protects you from the following damage types: Cold, Corrosion, Fire, Lightning, and Poison.

Mystical Damage Reduction: MDR protects you from the following damage types: Radiant, Psychic, and Umbral.

DC Tip: There can be specific types of DR for certain Damage Types that special items or monsters have. You could have something that even gives "Fire Damage Reduction" for example.

Example: A creature makes an Attack to hit a target with an PD of 10. The result of their Check is 12, which hits the target and deals 4 Bludgeoning damage. The target has a PDR, which provides Bludgeoning Resistance against this Attack, and halves the damage to 2 (4 / 2 = 2). Alternatively, if the result of the Check was 15, it would be 5 over the target's PD of 10, making it a Heavy Hit. In this scenario, the PDR is bypassed, the damage isn't reduced, and the target takes the full 4 damage plus 1 damage for the Heavy Hit.

heading: Stacking Damage Vulnerabilities, Resistances, & Immunities
text: ## Stacking Damage Vulnerabilities, Resistances, & Immunities

heading: Damage Resistance X & Vulnerability X
text:
## Damage Resistance X & Vulnerability X

A creature that gains Damage Resistance X or Damage Vulnerability X from multiple sources they are all added together.

heading: Damage Resistance (Half) & Vulnerability (Double)
text:
## Damage Resistance (Half) & Vulnerability (Double)

Multiple sources of Damage Resistance (Half) or Damage Vulnerability (Double) do not stack.

Example: If you had Lightning Resistance (Half) from a Class Feature and Elemental Resistance (which includes Lightning Resistance) from the EDR granted by your Armor, you would still only take half damage when you take Lightning damage (not a quarter of the damage, because they don't stack).

heading: Bonus Damage
text:
## Bonus Damage

If an Attack gains a bonus to its damage (such as from a Feature or Enhancement), the bonus damage is the same type of damage as the Attack, unless otherwise stated.

heading: Multiple Damage Types
text:
## Multiple Damage Types

When an Attack deals multiple types of damage and gains a bonus to its damage, you choose which damage type gains the bonus damage.

Example: A Barbarian Hits a target with an Attack using a magical Hammer that deals 2 Bludgeoning damage and 2 Lightning damage. If the Barbarian is Raging at the time, they deal +1 damage from their Rage Feature. The Barbarian can choose to apply their Rage damage to the Bludgeoning or Lightning damage.

heading: Against Multiple Targets
text:
## Against Multiple Targets

If an Attack that targets multiple targets gains a bonus to its damage, the bonus damage only applies to 1 target of your choice.

DC Tip: The Attack still benefits from Critical Hits, Heavy Hits, and beyond against each target.

heading: Damage Modifications
text:
## Damage Modifications

Total damage is determined after all damage modifications are taken into account. When a creature takes damage, the total damage taken is determined by following the steps below:

heading: Adding & Subtracting
text:
## Adding & Subtracting

- Damage Resistance X: its Resist X reduces the damage by the value of X (1, 2, 3, etc.)
- Damage Vulnerability X: its Vulnerable X increases the damage by the value of X (1, 2, 3, etc.)

heading: Doubling & Halving
text:
## Doubling & Halving

- Damage Resistance (Half): The damage is halved (rounded up, minimum of 1 damage reduced).
- Damage Vulnerability (Double): The damage is doubled (minimum of 1 damage taken).

If a creature has the same type and amount of Resistance and Vulnerability, they cancel each other out (e.g. Fire Resistance (1) & Fire Vulnerability (1), or Cold Resistance (Double) & Cold Vulnerability (Double)).

DC Tip: Basically any kind of addition or subtraction of damage happens FIRST, then any multiplication or division of damage AFTER.

Example 1: A creature has Cold Resistance (1) from its Ancestry, and Cold Vulnerability (Double) from a Monster Effect. When that creature is Hit by an Attack that deals 4 Cold damage, the damage is reduced by 1 (Cold Resistance (1)) to a total of 3, and then doubled (Cold Vulnerability) to a total of 6.

Example 2: A creature has Fire Vulnerability (1) from its Ancestry, and Fire Resistance (Half) from the EDR granted by its Armor. When that creature is Hit by an Attack that deals 5 Fire damage, the damage is increased by 1 (Fire Vulnerability (1)) to a total of 6, and then halved (Fire Resistance (Half) from EDR) to a total of 3.

heading: Managing Damage Multiples
text: ## Managing Damage Multiples

heading: Multiple Damage Types
text:
## Multiple Damage Types

When you take multiple types of damage that you have Resistances (or Vulnerabilities) to, you apply your Resistances (or Vulnerabilities) to each damage type individually.

Example: You have Physical Resistance (Half) from PDR granted by Armor and take 3 Piercing damage and 3 Slashing damage from an Attack. The Piercing damage would be reduced by half to 1.5, then rounded up to 2 damage taken. The same would be true of the Slashing damage (2 damage taken). You would take a total of 4 damage from the Attack.

heading: Bonus Damage Against Multiple Targets
text:
## Bonus Damage Against Multiple Targets

If you add bonus damage to an Attack that targets multiple targets, the bonus damage only applies to 1 target of your choice. The Attack still benefits from Critical Hits, Heavy Hits, and beyond against each target.

heading: Shared Damage
text:
## Shared Damage

When 2 or more creatures share damage, divide the damage by the number of creatures (rounding up), and distribute the damage equally among the creatures. If the creatures are sharing damage of multiple types, you calculate the shared damager for each type taken (see example 3)

Example 1: A Champion steps in front of an Attack against an ally and they share the damage. If the total damage was 5, then each creature now takes 3 damage (half of 5 is 2.5, rounded up to 3).

Example 2: A creature is thrown 10 Spaces into a wall, taking 10 Bludgeoning damage upon impact. However, if 2 creatures were to stand side-by-side in the thrown creature's path, all 3 creatures would share the damage upon impact. The 10 damage would be divided by the 3 creatures, resulting in each creature taking 3.33 Bludgeoning damage. No matter what the fraction is, you always round up in DC20, so now each creature takes 4 damage.

Example 3: A Barbarian steps in front of an Attack against an ally and they share the damage. If the total damage was 3 Bludgeoning and 2 Lightning damage, then each creature now takes 2 Bludgeoning (half of 3 is 1.5, rounded up to 2). and 1 Lightning damage.

DC Tip: Yes this does mean that sharing damage might result in the group as a whole taking more damage than if they had not shared the damage. This is on purpose and also accounts for everyone smashing into each other and truly sharing the blow.

<!-- page: 40 -->

heading: Combat Rules
text:
## Combat Rules

<!-- page: 41 -->

heading: Combat Resources
text: ## Combat Resources

heading: Action Points
text:
## Action Points

Action Points (AP) represent a character's moment-tomoment readiness in both battle. Each character has a maximum of 4 AP, which they can spend to perform Actions or Reactions. Whether it's striking with a blade, casting a spell, or leaping from danger's path, managing AP wisely is key to surviving fast-paced encounters and making the most of every opportunity.

heading: Maximum AP
text:
## Maximum AP

You have a maximum of 4 Action Points. You are considered to be taking an Action when you spend 1 or more Action Points. Once an Action Point is spent, it can't be used again until it's regained.

Example: A PC has 4 AP. They spend 1 AP to make an Attack with a Weapon they're wielding. They decide to spend 3 AP to improve their Attack: 1 AP to increase damage, 1 AP to increase their range, and 1 AP to gain ADV on the Attack. After spending all 4 AP, they can't take any more Actions until they regain their AP. See the "Martial Enhancements" on page 50 for more information on enhancing your Attacks.

heading: Actions out of Combat Sidebar
text:
## Actions out of Combat Sidebar

When you are not in Combat, there is no Initiative, so the GM decides how quickly time flows. They set the pace and determine how often creatures can take actions. In calm situations, you might have all the time you need, taking action after action without limit. In situations where time is short (such as a sneaking into a bandit's hideout), the GM may limit how many actions you can take to help track the order and urgency of events. You do not need to track movement the same way you do in Combat.

For example, nobody is counting every single Space someone walks while shopping in the market. Similarly, you do not spend Action Points out of Combat. You simply describe what you are doing. However, the number of Action Points you could spend in Combat can be a useful guideline for how much effort you can reasonably put into a single attempt at an activity.

Beta Note: We will be adding or changing the rules around Helping outside of Combat in a future update.

heading: Mana Points
text:
## Mana Points

<!-- page: 42 -->
Mana Points (MP) are the wellspring of magical power, allowing Spellcasters to unleash potent spells and supernatural effects. Unlike Stamina Points, MP can't be regained through action or effort during the day, instead fully replenishing only after a Long Rest. However, Spellcasters have a larger pool of MP than Martials do SP, and each Mana Point produces a greater effect than a Stamina Point, making every MP-fueled ability a more powerful expenditure of energy.

heading: MP Effects
text:
## MP Effects

Any ability or effect that costs MP is considered an MP Effect. This includes Spells, Class Features, or Talents that you spend MP to use.

heading: Maximum MP
text:
## Maximum MP

You don't have Mana Points unless you choose a Spellcaster Class (Bard, Cleric, Druid, Sorcerer, Spellblade*, Warlock, and Wizard) or progress in the Spellcaster Path (see "Character Paths" on page 192 for more information).

- Spellcaster Class: Spellcaster Classes have a maximum amount of MP as shown in the Mana Points column of their Spellcaster Class Table.
- Spellcaster Path: If you spend a Path point on the Spellcaster Path, you gain maximum amount of MP as shown on the Mana Points column of the Spellcaster Path Table.

heading: Mana Spend Limit
text:
## Mana Spend Limit

You can spend a maximum number of Mana Points to produce an MP Effect equal to your Combat Mastery. This limitation includes MP spent on Spell Enhancements and other Features that require MP. This is called your Mana Spend Limit (MSL).

Example: A level 6 Sorcerer (Combat Mastery of 3) can spend up to 3 MP on any Spell they cast. They could cast a Spell with a base cost of 3 MP, or they could cast a Spell with a base cost of 1 MP and spend 2 MP on Spell Enhancements. Alternatively, they could cast a Spell without an MP cost and spend 3 MP on Spell Enhancements.

DC Tip: The Mana Spend Limit is separate from the Stamina Spend Limit, meaning you could spend up to your MSL and up to you SSL on the same action, such as when using the Spellblade's Spellstrike Feature.

heading: Regaining MP
text:
## Regaining MP

You regain all spent MP when you complete a Long Rest.

heading: Stamina Points
text:
## Stamina Points

Stamina Points (SP) are a vital resource, empowering Martial characters to perform heroic feats beyond the limits of ordinary warriors. Each Martial Class has unique ways to regain SP, whether through relentless aggression, strategic maneuvers, or unwavering discipline, ensuring they can continue to push the limits of Combat.

heading: SP Effects
text:
## SP Effects

Any ability or effect that costs SP is considered an SP Effect. This includes Maneuvers, Class Features, or Talents that you spend SP to use.

heading: Maximum SP
text:
## Maximum SP

You don't have Stamina Points unless you choose a Martial Class (Barbarian, Champion, Commander, Hunter, Rogue, Monk, and Spellblade*) or progress in the Martial Path (see "Character Paths" on page 192 for more information).

- Martial Class: Martial Classes have a maximum amount of SP as shown in the Stamina Points column of their Martial Class Table.
- Martial Path: If you spend a Path point on the Martial Path, you gain maximum amount of SP as shown on the Stamina Points column of the Martial Path Table.

heading: Stamina Spend Limit
text:
## Stamina Spend Limit

You can spend a maximum number of Stamina Points to perform an SP Effect equal to your Combat Mastery. This limitation includes SP spent on Maneuver and Attack Enhancements and other Features that require SP. This is called your Stamina Spend Limit (SSL).

Example: A level 6 Barbarian (Combat Mastery of 3) can spend up to 3 SP on any SP Effect they perform. They could perform a Maneuver with a base cost of 3 SP, or they could perform a Maneuver with a base cost of 1 SP and spend 2 SP on Maneuver Enhancements. Alternatively, they could perform a Maneuver without an SP cost and spend 3 SP on Maneuver Enhancements.

DC Tip: The Stamina Spend Limit is separate from the Mana Spend Limit, meaning you could spend up to your SSL and up to you MSL on the same action, such as when using the Spellblade's Spellstrike Feature.

heading: Regaining SP
text:
## Regaining SP

You regain all spent SP when Combat ends and when you complete a Short Rest.

Martial Stamina Regen: You can regain SP during Combat in a variety of ways depending on the Stamina Regen benefit granted by your Class or from the Martial Path (see the "Character Paths" on page 192chapter for more information).

Example: The Champion Class regains SP by performing Maneuvers, the Barbarian regains SP by taking or scoring Heavy, Brutal, and Critical Hits, and the Rogue regains SP by hitting creatures that are affected by Conditions.

heading: Grit Points
text:
## Grit Points

Characters with a high Charisma Attribute have a strong force of will. Grit Points represent a Character being able to push through and go beyond their limits in difficult situations.

heading: Maximum Grit Points
text:
## Maximum Grit Points

You have a maximum number of Git Points equal to 2 + your Charisma (minimum of 0 Grit Points).

heading: Spending Grit Points
text:
## Spending Grit Points

Bearing Damage: When you're Hit by an Attack, you can spend 1 or more Grit Points to reduce the damage you take by 1 per Grit Point spent.

Save Resolve: When you make a Save, you can spend Grit Points to grant yourself ADV on the Save. You must do so before you roll any dice as part of making the Save.

heading: Regaining Grit Points
text:
## Regaining Grit Points

You regain all spent Grit Points when you complete a Long Rest.

<!-- page: 45 -->

heading: Actions
text:
## Actions

Anything that you spend Action Points on is considered an Action.

heading: Taking an Action
text:
## Taking an Action

A creature can take an Action on its turn by spending Action Points (AP). The AP cost of taking an Action is listed in the Action's description.

heading: Minor Action
text:
## Minor Action

Once per turn, you can perform up to 2 of the following tasks without spending AP. You can't take Actions (such as the Attack Action) between these 2 tasks.

- Open or Close a Door.
- Grab or Release an Item.
- Retrieve or Stow an Item.
- Draw or Sheathe a Weapon.

Additional Minor Actions: You can spend 1 AP to perform a Minor Action again during the same turn.

heading: Types of Actions
text:
## Types of Actions

A creature can take the following Actions on its turn: Attack, Move, Disengage, Dodge, Grapple, Help, Hide, Object, Shove, and Spell Action.

heading: Offensive Actions
text: ## Offensive Actions

heading: Attack
text:
## Attack

You can spend 1 AP to make 1 Attack Check.

heading: Disarm
text:
## Disarm

You can spend 1 AP to make an Attack Check contested by the target's Athletics, Acrobatics, or Trickery Check (target's choice), the target has ADV if they're holding the object with 2 hands, you have DisADV if the target is larger than you. You can't Disarm a creature that's 2 Sizes larger than you. Success: The targeted object falls into an unoccupied space of your choice within 1 Space of the creature.

heading: Grapple
text:
## Grapple

Using a free hand, you can spend 1 AP to attempt to Grapple another creature within 1 Space. Make an Athletics Check contested by the opposing creature's Acrobatics or Athletics Check. You or the target may have ADV or DisADV on your Check based on each other's size. Success: The creature is Grappled by you.

heading: Moving the Target
text:
## Moving the Target

You can move the target to any Space adjacent to you by spending your own Movement to do so. Alternatively, when you move, you can move the target with you, but you're considered Slowed (Every 1 Space you move costs an extra 1 Space of movement).

Multiple Grapplers: If you attempt to move or throw a target (see "Throw Creature" on page 57) that's also Grappled by 1 or more other creatures, you must make a Contested Athletics Check against all the other Grapplers. Success: You move or throw the target as normal and break the Grapple of all other creatures who were also Grappling that target.

heading: Ending a Grapple
text:
## Ending a Grapple

Escape Grapple: The target can spend 1 AP to attempt to free itself from the Grapple. When it does, it makes an Acrobatics or Athletics Check contested by your Athletics Check. Success: The Grapple immediately ends.

Incapacitated Grappler: If you become Incapacitated, the Grapple immediately ends.

Forced Movement: If an effect attempts to forcibly move the target beyond your reach, you make the Check or Save instead of the target. If the effect targets both you and the target, you make 1 Check or Save for both of you. Success: The targets of the effect are not moved. Failure: The Grapple immediately ends, and the targets of the effect are moved.

Falling: If the target begins falling (and you don't), the Grapple ends if you can't carry the target's weight. If you can carry its weight, you hold it suspended in the air.

heading: Shove
text:
## Shove

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to push a creature within 1 Space of you. Make an Athletics Check contested by the target's Acrobatics or Athletics Check. Success: You push the creature 1 Space away from you or to its left or right. Success (each 5): Push up to 1 additional Space.

Knock Prone: After the result, you can choose to reduce the total distance the target is pushed by 1 Space to knock them Prone instead.

DC Tip: If you shove a creature into a target, follow the rules for hitting the target in the Collision section.

heading: Tackle
text:
## Tackle

If you move at least 2 Spaces in a straight line, you can spend 1 AP to attempt to Tackle a creature that's your Size or smaller. Make an Athletics Check contested by the target's Acrobatics or Athletics Check. Success: You Grapple the target and you both move 1 Space in the same direction you're moving then immediately fall Prone. Success (each 5): +1 Space moved.

heading: Defensive Actions
text: ## Defensive Actions

heading: Disengage
text:
## Disengage

You can spend 1 AP to impose DisADV on Opportunity Attacks made against you until the start of your next turn.

Full Disengage: When you take the Disengage Action, you can spend an additional 1 AP to become Immune to Opportunity Attacks until the start of your next turn.

heading: Dodge
text:
## Dodge

You can spend 1 AP to impose DisADV on the next Attack or Grapple made against you before the start of your next turn.

Full Dodge: When you take the Dodge Action, you can spend an additional 1 AP to impose DisADV on all Attacks or Grapples made against you until the start of your next turn.

heading: Hide
text:
## Hide

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to Hide from 1 or more creatures that can't see you (Unseen). Make a Stealth Check against any opposing creatures' Passive Awareness. Success: You become Hidden from creatures whose Passive Awareness you beat (making you Unseen and Unheard by them). You remain Hidden until you make a noise louder than a whisper, make an Attack, cast a Spell with a Verbal Component, or a creature takes the Search Action and successfully locates you.

See “Hidden Creatures” on page 163 for more on hiding.

DC Tip: When you take the Hide Action and beat an enemy's Passive Awareness, you only become Hidden from that creature because it can't see or hear you (you are both Unseen and Unheard). However, if your Stealth Check beats one enemy's Awareness Score but not another's, then you are Hidden from the first enemy, but you're not Hidden from the second enemy.

heading: Utility Actions
text: ## Utility Actions

heading: Move
text:
## Move

You can spend 1 AP to move up to your Speed in Spaces (default of 5). You choose where to move, and can break up your movement by moving before and after taking a different Action. You can't end your turn in a Space occupied by another creature.

Example: A creature with 4 AP can take the Move Action to move 1 Space, take the Attack Action, move 1 Space again (2 total Spaces), take the Attack Action again, and then move 2 Spaces again (4 total Spaces).

heading: Help
text:
## Help

You can spend 1 AP to grant another creature a d8 Help Die that lasts until the start of your next turn. Upon granting the Help Die, you must declare which creature you're Helping and the type of Check you'll be aiding them with, while meeting the following conditions:

Attack: You declare 1 target for the Attack. You must be within 1 Space of the Attacker or the target of the Attack. While the Help Die lasts, it can be added to an Attack made against the target.

Skill or Trade Check: You declare a type of Skill or Trade Check. You describe how you're Helping them and must do so with a Skill or Trade that you have at least 1 Mastery Level in. You can use the same Skill or Trade or a different one.

The Help Die can only be used to aid the type of Check declared and disappears once it's used.

Multiple Help Penalty: Once you take the Help Action, each time you take the Help Action again before the end of your turn, your Help Die decay by 1 step, to a minimum of a d4 (d8 > d6 > d4). These Help Dice only decay when using the Help Action. Help Die granted by other sources decay independently of any Help Dice grant through the Help Action.

Example: A Barbarian is about to wrestle an alligator and will need to make an Athletics Checks. A Hunter with Mastery in the Animal Skill can grant the Barbarian a Help Die to add to their Athletics Check made to wrestle the alligator.

DC Tip: When taking the Help Action, the GM may allow the PC to contribute to a Skill or Trade Check by using something other than their own Skill or Trade Mastery. If the PC is a Wizard, they may try to use a Spell to contribute to another creature's Skill or Trade Check, but only if the GM decides that it makes sense to do so.

heading: Object
text:
## Object

You can spend 1 AP to perform 1 of the following object interactions:

- Drink a Potion or Administer a Potion to another Creature.
- Attempt to Lock or Unlock a Lock.
- Make a Trickery Check to activate or disable a trap or other mechanism.
- Transfer an Item to or from another Creature (only 1 of the two creatures spends 1 AP).
- Throw an Item to location you can see up to 5 Spaces away.

heading: Spell
text:
## Spell

You can spend 1 or more AP to cast a Spell that you know. If the Spell has a Mana Point requirement, you must spend that much MP to cast the Spell.

heading: Sustained Action
text:
## Sustained Action

You must spend 1 AP at the start of each of your turns to Sustain an effect until the start of your next turn. You can't Sustain an effect while Dazed. When you become Dazed, any effect you're Sustaining ends. You can choose to end the effect at anytime for free.

Outside of Combat: You can only Sustain 1 effect at a time outside of Combat. If you attempt to Sustain a second effect while already Sustaining a first, the first effect immediately ends.

heading: Skill Based Actions
text:
## Skill Based Actions

A creature can take the following Skill Actions on its turn: Analyze Creature, Calm Animal, Combat Insight, Conceal, Feint, Intimidate, Investigate, Jump, Medicine, Mounted Defense, Pass Through, or Search.

heading: Analyze Creature
text:
## Analyze Creature

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to recall or discern some information about a creature that you can see or hear. Make a DC 10 Knowledge Check.

Success: You learn a piece of lore about the creature. Success (each 5): You learn 1 creature statistic (PD, AD, Attacks, Abilities, Resistances, Vulnerabilities, Immunities, etc.)

DC Tip: There are 5 types of Knowledge Checks related to creature types: Arcana (Constructs, Monstrosities, and Oozes), History (Dragons, Giants, and Humanoids), Nature (Beasts, Elementals, Fey, and Plants), Occultism (Aberrations, Fiends, and Undead), and Religion (Celestials). If a PC has a custom Knowledge Trade they think might help in the situation, they can ask the GM to use that Knowledge Trade instead.

heading: Calm Animal
text:
## Calm Animal

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to beguile a Beast that can see or hear you. Make an Animal Check contested by the target's Repeated Charisma Save.

Success: The animal is Taunted by you for 1 minute or until you target it with a harmful Attack, Spell, or other effect. Success (5): It's instead Intimidated. Success (10): It's also Charmed.

heading: Combat Insight
text:
## Combat Insight

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to discern the course of actions a creature might take on its next turn. Make an Insight Check contested by the target's Trickery or Influence Check (its choice).

Success: You learn the target's emotional state and whether it plans to make an Attack, cast a Spell, or flee combat during its next turn. Success (5): You know who the creature is likely to target with a harmful ability. Success (10): You know which ability the creature plans to use.

DC Tip: If the target is a PC, its player tells you what the creature plans to do. If the target is an NPC, the GM tells you what the creature plans to do.

heading: Conceal
text:
## Conceal

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to hide an object on yourself or in nearby foliage, debris, or decor to render it Hidden. Make a contested Trickery Check against the Passive Awareness of creatures that can see you.

Success: The object is Hidden from any creature whose Passive Awareness you beat.

heading: Feint
text:
## Feint

You can spend 1 AP to make a Trickery Check contested by the Insight Check of a creature that can see you within 3 Spaces.

Success: The next Attack against the target before the start of your next turn has ADV and deals +1 damage.

heading: Intimidate
text:
## Intimidate

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to intimidate a creature that can see or hear you within 10 Spaces. Make an Intimidation Check contested by the target's Charisma Save. Success: The target is Intimidated by you for 1 Round.

heading: Investigate
text:
## Investigate

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to uncover a concealed object on a creature, a secret compartment, or the intended function of a mechanism within 1 Space of you.

Concealed Objects: You can attempt to uncover any objects concealed on a creature. Make an Investigation Check contested by the target's Trickery Check.

Success: You know the location of any concealed object on the creature.

Secret Compartments: You can attempt to uncover any secret comportments. Make an Investigation Check against the discovery DC of any secret compartments.

Success: You discover the location of any secret compartments whose discovery DC you beat.

Discern Mechanism: You can attempt to discern the functionality of a mechanism (the effect of a trap, how to open a secret door, or activate a device). Make an Investigation Check.

Success: You learn how the mechanism works and the methods to activate and disable it (if any).

heading: Extend Jump
text:
## Extend Jump

When you perform a Jump, you can spend 1 AP to increase your Jump Distance for the Jump. Make a DC 10 Acrobatics Check. Failure: Your Jump Distance increases by 1. Success: Your Jump Distance increases by 2. Success (each 5): +1 Jump Distance.

DC Tip: Remember, your Long Jump Distance equals your Jump Distance in Spaces, and your High Jump distance equals your Jump Distance in feet.

heading: Medicine
text:
## Medicine

You can spend 1 AP to touch a creature and tend to its wounds. Make a DC 10 Medicine Check. Success: You end 1 stack of Bleeding on the target. Success (each 5): You end 1 additional stack of Bleeding on the target.

<!-- page: 46 -->

heading: Mounted Defense
text:
## Mounted Defense

You can spend 1 AP to maneuver a mount you're riding to avoid danger. Make a DC 10 Animal Check.

Success: The mount's PD increases by 2 until the start of your next turn. Success (5): +2 PD. Success (10): +4 PD.

heading: Pass Through
text:
## Pass Through

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to move through a Space occupied by a hostile creature that's within 1 size of you. Make a contested Martial Check against the target. Success: You can move through the creature's Space as if it were Difficult Terrain (Slowed while moving through the area). Success (5): You can move through the creature's space without it counting as Difficult Terrain.

heading: Search
text:
## Search

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to locate 1 or more Hidden creatures and concealed objects within your Line of Sight.

Hidden Creatures: You attempt to locate any Hidden creatures in the area. Make an Awareness Check against the Stealth Check of any Hidden creatures. Success: You know the location of any Hidden creature whose Stealth Check you beat until the end of your turn. Knowing a creature's location doesn't grant you the ability to see it.

Hidden Objects: You attempt to locate any Hidden objects in the area. Make an Awareness Check against the DC to discover any concealed objects (such as traps, secret doors, or hidden items). Success: You discover the location of any Hidden object whose discovery DC you beat.

DC Tip: Without obstruction, a PC's line of sight could reach the horizon. You might ask your PC to make their Awareness Check with Disadvantage to detect a creature that's far away, such as beyond 10 Spaces.

heading: Taunt
text:
## Taunt

You can spend 1 AP to attempt to taunt a creature that can see or hear you within 10 Spaces. Make an Influence Check contested by the target's Charisma Save. Success: The target is Taunted by you for 1 Round.

heading: Advanced Actions
text: ## Advanced Actions

heading: Held Actions
text:
## Held Actions

Sometimes the circumstances aren't exactly right for the action you want to take. When that happens, you can choose to wait until just the right moment by Holding your Action. To Hold your Action, you must declare an Action you want to take and state a Trigger. Once you do so, you can't change the Action or Trigger.

Declare Action: When you declare the Action, you must immediately spend the Action Points required to take that Action. The Multiple Check Penalty from this turn applies to any Checks you make as part of this Reaction.

State Trigger: The Trigger must be an observable event, such as "I want to wait until an enemy moves into range to make an Attack against them", "I want to wait until I see my ally get out of range before I shoot the barrel filled with explosives", or something else.

Take Reaction: If the stated Trigger occurs at any time before the start of your next turn, you can take the declared Action as a Reaction. If the Reaction requires you to spend any additional resources (such as Stamina Points or Mana Points) you spend them now. Your Reaction interrupts the events stated in the Trigger, allowing you to act before the events resolve. If the Trigger never occurs, then the declared Action can't be taken and the AP spent is wasted.

Example: It's your turn and you have 2 AP remaining to spend, but you really want to work together with an ally. You tell the GM that you want to spend 2 AP on Held Actions. You declare that you will be taking the Move Action and the Attack Action and you spend 2 AP right now. You also state the Trigger for these Held Actions is "your ally is in melee range of an enemy". Once the Trigger occurs you take those 2 Actions immediately. You take a Move Action to move to the creatures and then you make an Attack Action on a creature. If the creatures are too far away for 1 Move Action (1 AP), then you would only be able to move as far as you can and the Attack Action (1 AP) is wasted since you were not close enough to make an Attack. You can't convert the 1 AP spent on the Attack Action into another Move Action, since that's not what you declared.

DC Tip: PCs can set triggers to be something less specific like, "I'm going to shoot an arrow at the next enemy I see." Just make sure the PC and the GM are on the same page on what the intent of what they're wanting to do is for their Reactions Trigger.

<!-- page: 47 -->

heading: Reactions
text:
## Reactions

Reactions are Actions that a creature can take during another creature's turn. A creature can't take a Reaction on their own turn, unless the Reaction allows them to do so in response to another creature's Reaction. Reactions are not affected by the Multiple Check Penalty.

heading: Taking a Reaction
text:
## Taking a Reaction

A creature can take a Reaction on another creature's turn provided it meets the requirements to do so. The requirements to take a Reaction are listed in the Reaction's Prerequisite and Trigger statements. You can only take 1 Reaction per Trigger.

Example 1: When a creature leaves a Martial PC's reach, they would only be able to make 1 Opportunity Attack against the creature (they could still spend AP to perform Martial Enhancements with their Opportunity Attack).

Example 2: When an Attack is made within 1 Space of a Martial PC, the PC could take the Parry Maneuver against the Attack (1st trigger). If the Attack still Hits (2nd trigger), the PC could take the Protection Maneuver to share the damage with the target.

heading: Reaction Requirements
text:
## Reaction Requirements

Prerequisite: A prerequisite states the Feature (Martial Feature, Spellcasting Feature, or a different Feature) required to gain access to the Reaction.

Trigger: A trigger states the conditions that must be met in order for a creature to take the Reaction.

heading: Spending Resources
text:
## Spending Resources

If a Reaction has a resource cost (Action Points, Mana Points, Stamina Points, etc.), a creature must spend those resources to take the Reaction.

Regaining Resources: Reactions give a creature an opportunity to spend Action Points during another creature's turn. If a creature spends Action Points, they'll have less Action Points to spend on their next turn. Any spent Action Points are not regained until the end of a creature's turn.

Example: If you take a Reaction to spend 1 Action Point, on your next turn you will only have 3 Action Points to use (4 -1 = 3).

DC Tip: If you somehow manage to spend all 4 of your AP before your turn, then you would have 0 AP at the start of your next turn. You wouldn't be able to do anything, so your turn would immediately end and you'd get your 4 AP back.

Multiple Reactions: A creature can spend Action Points to take multiple Reactions provided they meet all the conditions and can spend enough resources to do so.

heading: Types of Reactions
text: ## Types of Reactions

heading: Opportunity Attack
text:
## Opportunity Attack

Prerequisite: Martial Path

Trigger: A creature you can see within your Melee Range uses its movement to leave your Melee Range, draws a Weapon or Spell Focus, picks an item up off the ground, or takes the Object Action.

Reaction: You can spend 1 AP to make a Melee Martial Attack against the provoking creature.

DC Tip: You can spend additional AP to gain ADV or to perform Maneuvers with the Attack.

heading: Spell Duel
text:
## Spell Duel

Prerequisite: Spellcasting Path

During a Spell Duel, the creature casting the Spell is referred to as the Caster and the opposing creature is referred to as the Challenger.

If multiple creatures attempt to initiate a Spell Duel, one of them must be chosen as the Challenger, while the others assist them through Combo Casting. Additional creatures can also choose to assist the Caster when a Spell Duel is initiated (see "Combo Spellcasting" on page 67 for more information).

Trigger: You see an MP Effect being cast, provided the Caster or a target is within 5 Spaces of you.

Reaction: You can spend an amount of AP and 1 or more MP (up to your Mana Spend Limit) to attempt to stop the Caster's MP Effect. The amount of AP spent is equal to the base AP cost of the MP Effect you're trying to stop.

heading: Contest
text:
## Contest

Make a Spell Check contested by the Spell Check or Spell Attack that the Caster makes to cast their MP Effect. If the Caster's MP Effect doesn't require a Check, they have to make a Spell Check as a Reaction as part of the Spell Duel. For the purposes of the Spell Duel, the Caster and Challenger each gain a bonus to their Check equal to twice the MP spent they spent respectively.

Beta Note: Since enemies don't have MP costs for their MP Effects, you can assume their MP Effects cost an amount of MP equal to half their Level (rounded up).

<!-- page: 48 -->

heading: Outcomes
text:
## Outcomes

Success: The Caster’s MP Effect fails and has no effect.

Failure: The Caster's MP Effect succeeds and takes effect. The Check bonus gained from MP spent doesn't apply to the MP Effect's Success and Failure Statements.

Tie: The Caster's MP Effect fails, has no effect, and you each roll on the Wild Magic Surge Table. The effect from the table lasts until 1 Round has passed.

Resources Lost: Whatever the result, each creature still spends all AP, MP, or other resources as normal.

Example: A Caster makes an MP based Spell Attack and a Challenger attempts to stop it. The Caster's Spell Attack cost 1 AP and 2 MP. The Challenger must spend 1 AP and at least 1 MP to attempt to stop the Spell Attack. The Caster makes an Attack Check (since its a Spell Attack) and the Challenger makes a Spell Check. The Caster gets a +4 bonus to their Attack Check (2 MP spent) and the Challenger gets a +2 bonus to their Spell Check (1 MP spent). Should the Caster win the Contest, their MP Effect takes effect but they don't include the +4 bonus from MP spent to determine if they score a Heavy Hit or higher.

heading: Wild Magic Table
text: ## Wild Magic Table

heading: D20 Wild Surge Outcome
text:
## D20 Wild Surge Outcome

- 1 You turn into a small creature with the stats of a Sheep (HP 2, PD & AD 5, Melee Attack +2, Damage 1).
- 2 A wave of magic explodes out from you. You take True damage equal to your Prime Modifier and creatures within 5 Spaces must succeed a Physical Save against your Save DC or take the same amount of damage.
- 3 You are Stunned 3.
- 4 You are overcome by a wave of lethargy. You have DisADV on all Checks and Saves.
- 5 You are Stunned 1.
- 6 You are Blinded and Deafened.
- 7 All living creatures become Invisible to you.
- 8 You gain a d4 penalty on all Checks and Saves.
- 9 You grow by 1 size, become 2 times heavier, and your Speed is reduced by 2.
- 10 A strong gravitational pull originates from you. All creatures within 5 Spaces must make a Might Save or be pulled 4 Spaces toward you.
- 11 Forceful winds shoot out from you in all directions. All creatures within 5 Spaces (except you) must make a Might Save or be pushed 4 Spaces away from you.
- 12 You grow by 1 Size, become one and a half times heavier, and your Speed is increased by 2.
- 13 You gain a d4 bonus on all Checks and Saves.
- 14 You gain a Truesight of 10 Spaces.
- 15 You become Invisible.
- 16 Your maximum AP increases by 1 and you gain 1 AP.
- 17 You become energized. You have ADV on all Checks and Saves.
- 18 You gain a surge of power, granting you +5 to all Spell Checks you make.
- 19 You overflow with life energy. You and creatures within 5 Spaces regain HP equal to your Prime Modifier.
- 20 You turn into a large creature with the statistics of a Young Purple Dragon, but without a Breath Weapon (HP 30, PD & AD 16, Attack +10, Damage 4, Fly Speed 6).

<!-- page: 49 -->

heading: Martial Chapter
text:
## Martial Chapter

Martial characters in DC20 don't merely fight - they wage war with precision, discipline, and skill that elevates them far beyond the capabilities of an ordinary sword-wielder. Their prowess is represented through a specialized combat system which includes:

- Maneuvers: Tactical feats of strength, speed, and finesse used to control the battlefield.
- Enhancements: Modifications to Maneuvers that add extra impact, reach, area effects, or debilitating conditions, or upgrades that empower Martial Attacks with increased damage, range, or area of effect.

heading: Maneuvers
text:
## Maneuvers

Maneuvers are the signature techniques of martial combatants; deliberate, high-impact actions that go beyond basic attacks. Whether it's a sweeping strike to knock enemies back or a sudden burst of speed to reposition, Maneuvers allow warriors to control the flow of battle with skill and force.

heading: Maneuvers Known
text:
## Maneuvers Known

Martial Classes (Barbarian, Champion, Commander, Hunter, Rogue, Monk, and Spellblade*) know a number of Maneuvers as shown in the Maneuvers Known column of their Class Tables, plus any Maneuvers learned from Class Features, Talents, or Martial Path progression (see "Character Paths" on page 192 for more information).

heading: Maneuver Resources
text:
## Maneuver Resources

Each Maneuver has a minimum number of Action Points (AP) that must be spent to perform it. Many Maneuvers also include a minimum number of Stamina Points (SP) that must be spent to perform them. This is known as the Base SP Cost of a Maneuver.

heading: Action Points
text:
## Action Points

Performing any Maneuver requires spending Action Points (AP). Maneuvers that affect a single target typically cost 1 AP, while those that affect multiple targets usually cost 2 AP.

heading: Stamina Points
text:
## Stamina Points

Some Maneuvers have an SP cost that you must spend to perform them. The maximum amount of SP you can spend on a single Maneuver must be equal to or lower than your Stamina Spend Limit (see "Combat Resources" on page 41 for more information on Stamina Points).

DC Tip: Remember, your Stamina Spend Limit is equal to your Combat Mastery.

heading: Maneuver Characteristics
text: ## Maneuver Characteristics

heading: Duration
text:
## Duration

Maneuvers can either be instantaneous or continuous in their duration, and some Maneuvers require you to Sustain them in order to keep them going (see the "Sustained Action" on page 44 for more information).

- Instantaneous: Instantaneous Maneuvers take effect the moment they're performed.
- Continuous: These Maneuvers last a specified amount of time. They often end early when the affected creature Succeeds on a subsequent Save against the effect, it or another creature within 1 Space performs an Action to end the effect, or some other method stated to end the effect occurs (such as breaking line of sight).
- Sustained: Some effects require you to Sustain them or they end.

heading: Range
text:
## Range

This is the distance within which a target of the Maneuver, or the origin of its Area of Effect, must be. When selecting the targets of your Maneuver, they must be within the listed Range and fulfill any other listed requirement of the Maneuver (such as being visible or capable of hearing you). If the chosen Maneuver uses an Area of Effect to determine its targets, the origin of the Maneuver must be within its listed Range (see "Areas of Effect" on page 157 for more information).

<!-- page: 50 -->

heading: Performing a Maneuver
text:
## Performing a Maneuver

In order to perform a Maneuver, you must know the Maneuver and spend the required amount of Resources (Action Points and Stamina Points). When you perform a Maneuver, you must make a Check (such as an Attack Check or Martial Check) to determine the Maneuver's degree of success or failure. Your Check is compared against the target's Defense (such as PD or AD), the Maneuver's DC, or the target's Contested Check.

heading: Enhancements
text:
## Enhancements

Enhancements amplify the impact of a character's offense, turning well-executed strikes into devastating blows. They can increase damage, extend range, affect wider areas, or inflict conditions, allowing martial characters to adapt to the shifting demands of battle.

heading: Maneuver Enhancements
text:
## Maneuver Enhancements

Maneuver Enhancements allow you to spend additional AP or SP to increase the Maneuver's potency (such as damage, duration, range, number of targets, or Area of Effect). Maneuver Enhancements are specific to each Maneuver and are included in a Maneuver's description.

heading: Performing a Maneuver Enhancement
text:
## Performing a Maneuver Enhancement

When you perform a Maneuver, you can spend additional SP to add a Maneuver Enhancement with the Maneuver. If the Maneuver requires a Check (such as an Attack Check or Martial Check), you must choose to add the Maneuver Enhancement before you make the Check. You can perform the same Maneuver Enhancement multiple times per Maneuver, unless stated otherwise.

heading: Martial Enhancements
text:
## Martial Enhancements

Martial Enhancements empower a character's Martial Attacks with added force and versatility. By spending Action Points, a combatant can strike harder, reach farther, or break a foe's focus. These enhancements allow martial characters to elevate even their simplest strikes into potent tools of war, adapting on the fly to pressure, positioning, or opportunity. Some Attack Enhancements require a certain type of Weapon to perform (see "Equipment" on page 164 for more information).

Example: When you make an Attack with an Axe against 3 creatures using the Whirlwind Maneuver, you can spend 1 AP to add the Martial Enhancement: Damage and 1 AP to add the Attack Enhancement: Bleeding available only to Axes. 1 target of your choice takes +1 damage from the Attack and another target of your choice (can be the same target) must make a Save against your Save DC to avoid Bleeding.

heading: Performing a Martial Enhancement
text:
## Performing a Martial Enhancement

When you make a Martial Attack, you can spend 1 additional AP or SP to add a Martial Enhancement to the Attack. You must choose to add the Martial Enhancement before you make the Attack Check. You can perform the same Enhancement multiple times per Attack, unless stated otherwise.

DC Tip: Don't forget anyone can spend an additional 1 AP to gain ADV on their Attack Check.

heading: Martial Enhancements
text:
## Martial Enhancements

All characters with Weapon Combat Training gain access to the following Martial Enhancements:

- Damage: The Attack deals +1 damage against 1 target of the Attack.
- Range: The Attack's Range increase by 1 Space if it's a Melee Attack, or by 5 Spaces if it's a Ranged Attack. This Enhancement can only be performed once per attack.
- Daze: A creature of your choice targeted by the Attack makes a Might Save against your Save DC. Save Failure: The target becomes Dazed until the end of their next turn.

DC Tip: Remember, a Dazed creature can't Sustain any effects at the start of their turn! While Martial characters can't stop a Spell from being cast like a Spellcaster can with a Spell Duel, a Martial Character can more easily disrupt a Sustained Spell by causing the Spellcaster to lose focus.

Example: You spend 2 AP and 1 SP on an Attack against an enemy creature. 1 AP to take the Attack Action (make 1 Attack Check), 1 AP to add the Martial Enhancement: Damage to deal +1 damage, and 1 SP to add the Martial Enhancement: Range to grant your Attack some extra range.

heading: Categorizing Maneuvers
text: ## Categorizing Maneuvers

heading: Maneuver Types
text:
## Maneuver Types

There are several types of Maneuvers that allow you to produce a variety of powerful effects.

heading: Attack
text:
## Attack

Focused on delivering powerful, aggressive strikes, Attack Maneuvers deal damage, and are the core of a martial character's offensive toolkit—optimized for impact, precision, or area control.
